bio.education: Grafton High School (Grafton, Mass.) ‘12 University of Hartford ‘16, Criminal Justice & Psychology
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • A state champion runner in high school, Chaz Davis noticed a debilitating brightness in his right eye while sitting in class in his freshman year at the University of Harford. After completing his first cross country season, Davis was eventually diagnosed with leber’s hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON), a rare disease that destroys the optic nerve in the eye and can result in sudden vision loss. After a hiatus from running, Davis returned to the sport after his college roommates offered to be his guide runners in a 5-mile race in his hometown where proceeds would benefit LHON research. That turning point led him to compete in his collegiate track and cross country programs as well as earn a nomination to his first U.S. Paralympic Track & Field Team for the Rio 2016 Paralympic Games.
  • Son of Marquis and Audrey Davis
  • Has one younger brother, Austin who followed in Chaz’s footsteps and currently attends the University of Hartford
  • Owns a dog named Scooter and two cats, named George and Tango
  • Pursuing a master’s degree in social work and hopes to become a licensed social worker, therapist or a similar occupation in the field
  • Enjoys music and traveling.
Career Highlights
  • 2016 U.S. Paralympic Team Trials – Track & Field, gold (1500m, 5000m)
  • Toronto 2015 Parapan American Games, 2nd (5000m)
  • 2015 U.S. Paralympics Track & Field National Championships, 1st (5000m)
Paralympic Experience

    • Paralympic Games Rio de Janeiro 2016, 8th (5000m T13 - Men), 10th (1500m T13 - Men)

bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • A combat-wounded veteran who has been cycling since 2012
  • He joined the U.S. Army in 2002 and reached the rank of Staff Sergeant
  • On June 3, 2006, he rode shotgun in a Humvee as it drove through Ramadi, the most dangerous city in Iraq at the time
  • While turning, the truck ran over an improvised explosive device
  • The blast threw the truck two stories in the air and it flipped backward and landed on its roof
  • After surgery, he decided to have his left leg removed
  • Lives with his wife, Jamie, and four children, two daughters, Elliana and Lyllian, and two sons, Brenan and Isaiah in Fremont, Indiana, a small town of roughly 2,000 people
  • Son of Raymond and Wilma Davis
  • Has one sister, Pam, and one older brother, Joe
  • In his spare time, he enjoys coaching youth sports.

USA Basketball

Education:

Hamilton Southeastern High School (Fishers, Ind.) '13 University of Notre Dame '17

Quick Facts

  • Specialty: Long jump

Biographical Information

Tara Davis-Woodhall is a track and field athlete, specializing in hurdling and the long jump Born to a former track and field athlete (her father), she was destined for greatness in the sport and began running at the young age of four. A graduate of Agoura High School, where she first competed in track and field, Davis-Woodhall went on to attend the University of Georgia and then transferred to the University of Texas.

Outside of her life on the track, Davis-Woodhall runs a successful YouTube channel. 

Olympic Highlights

Tara Davis-Woodhall qualified for the Tokyo 2020 Olympic Games with a record of 7.04 meters in the long jump event. She competed in the finals and secured the 6th position with a jump of 6.84 meters.

In the Paris 2024 Olympic Games, she beat out the defending long jump champion to win gold, becoming the fourth American to place first in the long jump event.

World Championship Highlights

Davis-Woodhall finished first place in the World Indoor Championships in Glasgow in 2024 with a jump of 7.07 meters. In the World Athletics Championships held in Budapest in 2023, she won the silver medal in the long jump event. 

U.S. Championship Highlights

In 2023, Davis-Woodhall won the national long jump title at the USA Track and Field Championships in Albuquerque with a jump of 6.99 meters. In 2021, she set a collegiate record at the Texas Relays by jumping 7.14 meters.

Early Career

In her early years, Davis-Woodhall broke the high school record for the indoor long jump at the Dumanis Sports Group Prep Classic in Frisco, TX in 2017. At the CIF California State Meet in 2017, she won three gold medals and set a national high school record in the 100m hurdles with a time of 12.83 seconds. Her state record in the long jump also surpassed the 24-year-old record held by Olympian Marion Jones. 

In 2017, she was named Ventura County Track and Field Athlete of the Year, running the 100m hurdles in 12.89 seconds.

  • Years of participation: Long Jump - Women 2023

Career Highlights

  • 2008: No. 9 UNC jersey was retired in February, only the third player in program history to have her jersey retired
  • 2007: Recognized with the Honda Award as the National Player of the Year for field hockey, Selected as one of the five finalists for the Honda-Broderick Cup as the nation's top college female athlete, With 19 goals, ranked as the top scorer for the No. 1 offensive team in the country (UNC), Named 2007 ACC Defensive Player of the Year and 2007 ACC Tournament MVP, National Player of the Year by womensfieldhockey.com
  • 2006: Nominated for the 2006 WorldHockey Women's Young Player of the Year award as one of the top six players under the age of 23 in the world, Redshirted the 2006 season at UNC while representing the U.S. in international competition

More
From her childhood home in Berlin, NJ, Dawson shared her competitive spirit, natural athleticism and a tiny bathroom with her seven siblings. Two of her older sisters even played field hockey for the U.S. National Team. For Dawson, being a part of a big team is second nature. The versatile defender, midfielder joined the National team in 2005 and graduated from the University of North Carolina in 2007. She is the most capped athlete on the team with 240 marks. Dawson is a two-time Olympian.

Quick Facts
  • Speciality: Long Distances, Team Pursuit 

  • Took up speedskating at age 11

Biographical Information

Casey Dawson grew up in Park City, Utah, and took up speedskating at age 11 through a Youth Sports Alliance program at a local club.

Seven years later, Dawson made the national team, and within a few years, he qualified for the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, where he won a bronze medal in the team pursuit.

At age 25, Dawson won his first individual ISU World Cup event in the 5,000m, setting the U.S. record in the process, the weekend after he and his teammates broke the world record in team pursuit at the Salt Lake City World Cup.

After setting that world record in his home state, Dawson said, “My goal, other than going to the Olympics, is to keep inspiring the next generation. I was the kid 10 years ago watching people break world records … After the race, there was a line of kids waiting to get our signatures. It was great because, who knows, we might see those kids skating in the next couple of years and  in the 2034 Olympics here in Salt Lake.”

Olympic Experience
  • 2-time Olympian; 2-time Olympic medalist (1 silver, 1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Milan 2026, silver (Team Pursuit 8 Laps - Men), 8th (5000m - Men), 29th (1500m - Men)
    • Olympic Games Beijing 2022, bronze (Team Pursuit 8 Laps - Men), 28th (1500m - Men)
World Championships Experience

  • Medals: 1 (1 gold)

Quick Facts

Son of Marian Appuliese and Joseph DeFrancesco, Sebastian DeFrancesco is the eldest brother to three sisters, Alicia, Anne and Emily. He studied theater arts at Cabrillo College. His love of sport has extended to his daily life as he works with disabled children to promote sports. He is also a member of the Wheelchair Sports Hall of Fame and New England Hall of Fame. When he’s not competing, he is a playwright and occasional actor. He spent time serving in Green Berets with the US Army. In his free time, DeFrancesco enjoys sailing, painting and cooking.

Quick Facts
  • Is a native of Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic

  • Arrived to the United States in 1986 and worked at NYU as a graphic designer

  • After the 9/11 attack, he joined the Army

  • On Oct. 20, 2009 while serving in Helmand Providence in Afghanistan, he was wounded in action when the vehicle in which he was riding in was hit by rocket fire. As a consequence of the injury, he lost his right leg above the knee

  • Took up handcycling as part of his rehabilitation

  • Has a true passion for art, whether the medium is graphic design or abstract painting

  • Paints abstract art to illustrate the psychological experiences he faced in combat

  • Married to Jeanette De Los Santos.
